metadata.raw_background: I am from Macau, China, and I am currently majoring in Artificial Intelligence. I have been studying at HKUST(GZ) for two years since 2024.

responses.question: Could you briefly introduce yourself — your nationality, program, year of study, and how long you have been at HKUST(GZ)?
responses.answer: I am from Macau, China, and I am currently majoring in Artificial Intelligence. I have been studying at HKUST(GZ) for two years since 2024.

responses.question: What was your first impression of student life at HKUST(GZ), especially socially?
responses.answer: My first impression was that student life here is quite free and diverse. There are many chances to meet different people, including both Chinese students and international students. I felt that the campus gives students opportunities to experience different cultures.

responses.question: How easy or difficult was it for you to make friends when you first arrived? Why?
responses.answer: I think it was difficult for me to make friends when I first arrived. I am quite introverted when I enter a new environment. Also, I felt that the habits and lifestyles of people from different regions and countries were quite different from mine, so I did not make many friends at the beginning.

responses.question: Do you feel that you belong socially at HKUST(GZ)? Why or why not?
responses.answer: Not really. I still find it hard to fully fit into social groups at HKUST(GZ). I have a small circle of acquaintances, but I rarely take part in campus events or group activities. I usually prefer being alone, so I do not have a strong sense of social belonging here.

responses.question: Who do you mostly spend time with on campus — students from your own background, Chinese students, or a mix? What shaped that pattern?
responses.answer: Students from my own background. we share the same culture, language and life habits, so it is easier for us to communicate and understand each other. Also, we have similar living experiences and can relate to each other’s pressure and worries. Besides, we often take the same courses and join the same groups, which gives us more chances to stay together.

responses.question: Have you ever felt left out, disconnected, or excluded in social situations on campus? Can you describe a specific example?
responses.answer: No, not really. Although I only have a small circle of acquaintances, I still keep in contact with my friends and can stay connected through social media. So I do not often feel clearly excluded.

responses.question: How would you describe your interactions with Chinese students at HKUST(GZ)?
responses.answer: I think it is quite surface-leve. We only have small talk about daily life and say hi when we met each other. There is not deep conversation or close personal connectionp. Most of the time, we remain casual acquaintances.

responses.question: What makes it easier to connect with Chinese students, and what makes it harder?
responses.answer:
Some things make basic communication easier. We study on the same campus, take some of the same courses, and sometimes work together in group tasks. In daily life, most Chinese students are polite and friendly, so normal interaction is not difficult.
What makes it harder is that our backgrounds, habits, and interests are often different. Because of that, it is not always easy to find common topics for deeper conversations. Also, many students already have their own friend groups, so there are fewer chances to build closer connections.

responses.question: To what extent do language differences affect your social relationships here?
responses.answer: For me, language is not a big problem when communicating with Chinese students because I can speak Mandarin and Cantonese fluently. So in that sense, I do not feel much barrier. But when talking with some international students, English can become a difficulty for me because I am not very confident in spoken English. That sometimes affects communication, although in general we do not interact that much.

responses.question: To what extent do cultural differences affect your comfort, confidence, or communication with others?
responses.answer: I think cultural difference affects me quite a lot. People may have different communication styles, daily habits, and ways of thinking, and that can make interaction less natural. Sometimes I feel less comfortable or less confident when talking to others because I am not sure how they will respond or whether we understand each other in the same way. So culture does influence how easily I can communicate and build closer relationships.

responses.question: Do you think students at HKUST(GZ) mix naturally across backgrounds, or do they mostly stay in separate groups? Why?
responses.answer: I think students mostly stay in separate groups. From my experience, international students usually stay together, and I rarely see Chinese students included in those groups. So in daily campus life, people do not mix very naturally across different backgrounds.

responses.question: Do you think your social experience at university has affected your academic life? How?
responses.answer: Yes, I think it has affected my academic life. Because I mainly spend time with students from my own background, I do not communicate much with Chinese classmates about study. This means I have fewer chances to hear different ideas, share resources, or get useful study information from a wider group of people. It also makes me less active in discussions and group work, which affects my learning experience.

responses.question: Has your ability or inability to build friendships influenced your motivation, confidence, or study habits?
responses.answer: Yes, I think it has. Since my social connections are limited, I do not get much peer support or encouragement from a wider group of classmates. That can affect my motivation, especially when study pressure becomes heavy. It also affects my confidence in group discussion and classroom participation, because I do not always feel comfortable speaking up. In terms of study habits, I mostly rely on myself, so I have fewer chances to learn different methods from others.

responses.question: How has your social experience affected your access to study groups, academic help, or useful information?
responses.answer: It has affected it in a fairly direct way. Because my social circle is small, I do not easily enter study groups or wider academic networks. Sometimes useful information, like assignment reminders, course tips, or shared notes, spreads through classmates and friend groups. If you are not closely connected, you may get that information later than others, or you may miss it. I think that has happened to me sometimes.

responses.question: In group projects or collaborative assignments, have social or cultural dynamics affected your experience? Can you give an example?
responses.answer: Yes, they have. In some group projects, I felt less comfortable expressing my ideas, especially when other members already knew each other well. Their communication was smoother, while I needed more time to join in. Cultural difference can also affect the way people discuss things. Some students are more direct and active, while I am usually quieter. Because of that, my ideas may not be fully heard unless I make a stronger effort to speak.

responses.question: Have you ever felt academically disadvantaged because you were not well connected socially? Why or why not?
responses.answer: Yes, to some extent. Students with stronger social connections often find it easier to ask for help, exchange notes, or discuss class expectations. They may also know earlier about useful resources or opportunities. In my case, because I am not very socially connected, I sometimes need to solve problems by myself, and that can make academic work harder. I can still manage, but I do think there is a disadvantage.

responses.question: Do you think students with stronger social networks generally perform better academically here? What makes you say that?
responses.answer: In general, yes. Students with stronger social networks usually have more access to shared information, study support, and encouragement from others. They can ask questions more easily, join study groups, and get different opinions when they do not understand something. I think all of these things can help academic performance, especially in a university environment where cooperation is important.

responses.question: Have you noticed any difference in your performance between UCUG and UFUG courses? What do you think explains that difference?
responses.answer: Yes, I have noticed some difference. I usually feel more comfortable in UFUG courses. In UCUG courses, participation, communication, and interaction sometimes feel more demanding, and that can affect how actively I take part in class. I think the difference comes from the classroom atmosphere, the level of interaction required, and how confident I feel in that environment.

responses.question: Do factors like language, class participation, or social connections affect your grades in UCUG and UFUG courses differently? How?
responses.answer: Yes, I think they do. In UCUG courses, class participation and communication seem to have a stronger influence, so language confidence and social connection matter more. If I do not feel comfortable speaking or working closely with others, it can affect my classroom performance and group work. In UFUG courses, I feel more relaxed and more familiar with the environment, so these factors affect me less.

responses.question: What do you think are the main barriers preventing stronger integration between international and Chinese students at HKUST(GZ)?
responses.answer: I think the main barriers are cultural differences, language difficulties in some cases, and the fact that students already stay in fixed social circles. Even if people are friendly, that does not automatically mean they will become close. If students do not share similar habits, interests, or ways of communicating, it is harder to build deeper relationships. Also, there may not be enough situations where students really need to work closely together over time.

responses.question: Do you think the university does enough to help international students integrate socially and academically? Why or why not?
responses.answer: I think the university has made some effort, but it is still not enough. There are campus activities and events, but joining the same event does not always help students build real connection. Sometimes the interaction stays very basic and ends after the activity. I think more practical and continuous support is needed, especially in both social life and academic cooperation.

responses.question: What changes would you recommend to improve social integration and academic support for international students?
responses.answer: I think the university could create more chances for meaningful interaction between international and Chinese students. For example, it could organize mixed study groups, mentoring programs, or small discussion activities where students need to work together more closely. In class, teachers could also pay more attention to group arrangement so that students from different backgrounds have more real contact. That may help students build stronger connections over time.

responses.question: Is there anything about the international student experience at HKUST(GZ) that people usually overlook or misunderstand?
responses.answer: Yes. I think people sometimes assume that international students will naturally adapt as long as they are on the same campus as everyone else. But in reality, being in the same place does not mean students feel included. Some students may look fine from the outside, but they may still feel disconnected, uncertain, or unsupported in daily life and study. I think this part is often overlooked.

summary_bullets: International student from Macau, China, majoring in Artificial Intelligence, with two years at HKUST(GZ).
Found it difficult to make friends initially because of introversion and differences in habits and lifestyles.
Does not feel a strong sense of social belonging and mostly stays with students from the same background.
Describes interaction with Chinese students as mostly surface-level.
Says language is not a major issue with Chinese students, but English can be a barrier with some international students.
Thinks cultural differences strongly affect comfort and confidence in communication.
Believes social experience affects academic access, participation, information flow, and confidence.
Feels more comfortable in UFUG than in UCUG and recommends more sustained, mixed interaction structures.
